title: Classic Hummus
description: Smooth, creamy hummus — ready in minutes. Ice-cold water is the secret to a light, fluffy texture.

steps.title: Prep chickpeas
steps.content: Drain and rinse {i1}. For extra-smooth hummus, rub them between a clean towel to slip off the skins — optional but worth it.

steps.title: Blend tahini base
steps.content: Add {i2} and {i3} to the food processor. Blend for 1 minute until pale and creamy. This is the key step.

steps.title: Add aromatics
steps.content: Add {i4}, {i6}, and {i7}. Blend for another 30 seconds.

steps.title: Add chickpeas
steps.content: Add {i1} and blend for 1–2 minutes, scraping down the sides as needed.

steps.title: Loosen and finish
steps.content: With the processor running, drizzle in {i8} gradually to reach your preferred consistency. Blend another minute until very smooth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Transfer to a bowl, drizzle with {i5}, and add a pinch of paprika or cumin on top. Serve with flatbread or veg.

notes: Blending the tahini and lemon first (before adding chickpeas) is the single most important step for a creamy result. Adjust to taste: more lemon for brightness, more tahini for richness, more cold water for a lighter texture. Keeps in the fridge for up to 5 days.

title: Tzatziki
description: Classic Greek yogurt dip with cucumber, garlic, and fresh dill. Best made a few hours ahead so the flavours meld.

steps.title: Grate and drain cucumber
steps.content: Coarsely grate {i2} and place in a clean tea towel. Squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible — this is the most important step to avoid a watery dip.

steps.title: Combine
steps.content: In a bowl, mix {i1}, the drained cucumber, {i3}, {i4}, {i5}, and {i6}. Season with {i7} and a pinch of white pepper. Stir well.

steps.title: Rest in fridge
steps.content: Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before serving to let the flavours develop. Drizzle with a little extra olive oil to serve.

notes: Use strained Greek yogurt (like Fage) for the thickest result. Fresh mint works well in place of, or alongside, dill. For a more mellow garlic flavour, let the minced garlic sit in the lemon juice for 5 minutes before mixing in.

title: Classic Gazpacho
description: A chilled Spanish tomato soup — bright, refreshing, and ready in minutes.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper, deseeded and roughly chopped

steps.title: Soak the bread
steps.content: If using, tear {i9} into chunks and soak in {i10} for 5 minutes. This gives the soup body and a creamier texture.

steps.title: Blend everything
steps.content: Add {i1}, {i2}, {i3}, {i4}, {i5}, soaked bread (if using), {i6}, {i7}, and {i8} to a blender. Blend on high for about 1 minute until very smooth.

steps.title: Strain (optional)
steps.content: For a silky-smooth result, pass the soup through a fine mesh sieve, pressing with a spoon. Skip if you prefer a more rustic texture.

steps.title: Chill and serve
steps.content: Taste and adjust salt and vinegar. Transfer to a bowl or jar and refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving — it tastes much better cold.

notes: Use the ripest tomatoes you can find — in season is ideal. Toppings: a drizzle of olive oil, diced cucumber, croutons, or cherry tomato halves. Keeps in the fridge for up to 3 days.

title: Creamy White Bean Soup with Kale, Rosemary & Lemon
description: A vegan soup with a rich, silky texture from partial blending — deeply satisfying without being heavy.

ingredients.name: cooked white beans (cannellini or butter beans), drained

steps.title: Sauté aromatics
steps.content: Heat {i1} in a large pot over medium heat. Add {i2} and cook for 5–6 minutes until soft. Add {i3} and {i4} and cook for another 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add beans and stock
steps.content: Add {i5} and {i6}. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes.

steps.title: Partial blend
steps.content: Remove the rosemary sprigs. Use an immersion blender to blend about half the soup directly in the pot — this creates creaminess while keeping texture.

steps.title: Add kale
steps.content: Add {i7} to the soup and simmer for 3–4 minutes until just wilted and bright green.

steps.title: Finish
steps.content: Add {i8}, adjust salt, and serve with a drizzle of olive oil and a pinch of Aleppo pepper or chilli flakes.

notes: Aleppo pepper gives a mild, fruity heat — regular chilli flakes work fine but use less. The partial-blending technique is the key to the creamy texture without any dairy.

title: Red Lentil & Zucchini Tikka Masala
description: A rich, creamy curry using Patak's Tikka Masala paste — ready in under an hour.

steps.title: Sauté base
steps.content: Heat {i1} in a large pot over medium heat. Add {i2} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add {i3} and {i4}, cook for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add paste
steps.content: Add {i5} and stir to coat the onions. Cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Add lentils, beans and tomatoes
steps.content: Add {i6}, {i7}, {i8}, {i9}, and {i12}. Stir well and bring to a boil.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.content: Reduce heat and simmer for 20–25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until lentils are soft and the curry has thickened.

steps.title: Finish with cream
steps.content: Stir in {i10}, {i11}, and {i13}. Simmer for another 5 minutes. Taste and adjust with {i14}.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Serve with wholegrain jasmine rice and naan. Top with fresh coriander if available.

notes: Nutritional yeast adds umami depth — don't skip it. Lentils continue to thicken as they sit — add a splash of water when reheating.

title: Spinach & Feta Byrek
description: Albanian-style savoury filo pastry — crisp, golden, and generously filled.

steps.title: Wilt and drain spinach
steps.content: Wilt {i1} in a hot dry pan. Once cooled, squeeze out every bit of moisture using a clean tea towel — this is the most critical step to avoid a soggy base.

steps.title: Make the filling
steps.content: Roughly chop the drained spinach. In a bowl, combine with {i2}, {i3}, {i4}, {i5}, {i6}, {i9}, and {i10}. Mix well.

steps.title: Layer the filo
steps.content: Preheat oven to 190°C. Brush a 30×20 cm baking dish with some of the {i7}. Lay 2–3 sheets of filo, brushing each with oil and letting the edges hang over the sides.

steps.title: Fill and top
steps.content: Spread the filling evenly over the filo base. Layer remaining filo sheets on top, brushing each with the remaining {i7}. Fold in the overhanging edges and brush the top generously. Score the top layer into portions before baking.

steps.title: Bake
steps.content: Bake at 190°C for 35–40 minutes until deep golden and crisp. Rest for 10 minutes before cutting.

notes: Keep unused filo sheets covered with a damp towel to prevent drying out. Frozen spinach works well — thaw and squeeze thoroughly. Reheats nicely in an oven at 180°C for 10 minutes.

title: Green Pea & Chickpea Fritters with Polenta Fries
description: Crispy polenta fries alongside herby green pea and chickpea flour fritters — great for dinner or a satisfying weekend lunch.

ingredients.name: fresh herbs (parsley, mint, or dill), chopped

steps.title: Make polenta slab
steps.content: Bring {i2} to a boil. Whisk in {i1} and stir constantly for 3–4 minutes until thick. Stir in {i3} and {i4}. Pour into a lightly oiled baking dish or tray to about 2cm thickness. Smooth the top and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes until firm and set.

steps.title: Make garlic yoghurt dip
steps.content: Mix {i13}, {i14}, {i15}, and {i16} together in a small bowl. Taste and adjust seasoning. Set aside in the fridge.

steps.title: Make fritter batter
steps.content: Roughly mash {i5} — keep it chunky, not smooth. In a bowl, combine mashed peas, {i6}, {i7}, {i8}, {i9}, {i10}, and {i11}. Mix until a thick batter forms. Rest for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Cut and bake polenta fries
steps.content: Preheat oven to 220°C. Cut the set polenta into thick finger-width fries. Place on a baking tray, drizzle with olive oil, and bake for 25–30 minutes, flipping halfway, until golden and crispy at the edges.

steps.title: Fry the fritters
steps.content: Heat {i12} in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Drop heaped spoonfuls of batter (about 60g each) and gently flatten. Cook for 3–4 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Work in batches.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Plate the polenta fries alongside the fritters. Serve the garlic yoghurt dip generously on the side.

notes: The polenta needs at least 30 minutes in the fridge to set firm enough to slice — make it ahead if possible, even the night before. Chickpea flour makes the fritters naturally gluten-free and gives a satisfying density.

title: Sweet Polenta Porridge
description: A warming, creamy breakfast porridge — quick to make and endlessly customisable with toppings.

ingredients.name: oat milk (or 350g oat milk + 150g oat cooking cream for richer result)

steps.title: Heat the liquid
steps.content: Pour {i2} into a medium saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a gentle simmer — don't boil.

steps.title: Whisk in polenta
steps.content: Slowly pour in {i1} while whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Keep whisking for 2–3 minutes until the mixture thickens to a creamy porridge consistency.

steps.title: Season
steps.content: Stir in {i3}, {i4}, {i5}, and {i6}. Taste and adjust sweetness.

steps.title: Serve immediately
steps.content: Spoon into bowls and add toppings of choice — fresh fruit, a spoonful of nut butter, toasted nuts, or the cranberry compote from this collection.

notes: Polenta thickens fast as it cools — serve immediately or keep on very low heat, stirring occasionally. If using oat cooking cream instead of part of the oat milk, the mixture thickens faster so watch the heat. Leftovers can be refrigerated and reheated with a splash of extra oat milk.

title: Moist Banana Bread
description: Buttery, tender, and deeply flavoured — Greek yogurt is the secret to keeping it perfectly moist.

steps.title: Preheat oven
steps.content: Preheat oven to 175°C. Grease a standard loaf pan (roughly 23×13 cm) and line the bottom with baking paper.

steps.title: Mash bananas
steps.content: Mash {i1} thoroughly in a large bowl. A few small lumps are fine.

steps.title: Mix wet ingredients
steps.content: Add {i2}, {i3}, {i4}, {i5}, and {i6} to the mashed bananas. Whisk until smooth and well combined.

steps.title: Add dry ingredients
steps.content: Add {i7}, {i8}, {i9}, and {i10} directly to the bowl. Fold gently with a spatula until just combined — do not overmix. Fold in {i11} if using.

steps.title: Bake
steps.content: Pour into the prepared loaf pan and bake for 55–65 minutes, until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. If the top browns too quickly, tent loosely with foil after 40 minutes.

steps.title: Cool
steps.content: Cool in the pan for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Slice once fully cooled for cleanest results.

notes: The riper the bananas, the sweeter and more flavourful. If you only have underripe bananas, roast them unpeeled at 180°C for 15 minutes until the skins blacken. Sour cream works as a substitute for Greek yogurt. Keeps at room temperature for 3 days, or freeze sliced.

title: Green Yoghurt Blueberry Lemon Muffins
description: Fluffy, tangy muffins packed with blueberries and bright lemon — with a spinach boost that keeps them moist and gives a subtle green hue without any grassy flavour.

steps.title: Preheat oven
steps.content: Heat oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Line a 12-hole muffin tin with paper cases or grease well.

steps.title: Blend the green base
steps.content: Blend {i1} with {i2} and the juice of {i3} until completely smooth and vivid green. Blending well is key — no spinach texture, beautiful colour.

steps.title: Mix wet ingredients
steps.content: Whisk {i4}, {i5}, {i6}, {i7}, and the zest of {i3} into the green yoghurt mixture until smooth.

steps.title: Combine dry ingredients
steps.content: In a large bowl, whisk together {i8}, {i9}, {i10}, and {i11}.

steps.title: Coat the blueberries
steps.content: Toss {i12} with {i13} in a small bowl. This helps prevent them from sinking.

steps.title: Fold together
steps.content: Pour the wet mixture into the dry ingredients and fold with a spatula until just combined — a few streaks of flour are fine. Do not overmix. Gently fold in the floured blueberries.

steps.title: Bake
steps.content: Divide batter evenly between the 12 cases. Bake for 18–22 minutes until risen, golden, and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack.

notes: The spinach flavour is completely undetectable — it only adds moisture and colour. Frozen blueberries work just as well as fresh; no need to thaw. Best eaten the day of baking, but keep well for 2 days in an airtight container.

title: Argentine Pepas (Quince Thumbprint Cookies)
description: The classic Argentine pepa — tender, buttery, melt-in-your-mouth dough with sweet-tart quince filling.

steps.title: Mix dry ingredients
steps.content: Whisk together {i1}, {i2}, and {i3} in a bowl. Set aside.

steps.title: Cream butter and sugar
steps.content: Beat {i4} and {i5} with a hand mixer for 2–3 minutes until pale and fluffy.

steps.title: Add wet ingredients
steps.content: Add {i6}, {i7}, {i8}, and {i9} to the butter mixture. Mix until well combined.

steps.title: Combine and chill
steps.content: Add the dry ingredients and fold until just combined — don't overmix. The dough will be sticky. Wrap in cling film, flatten into a disc, and chill in the fridge for at least 1 hour (overnight is even better).

steps.title: Prepare the quince filling
steps.content: Add {i10} and {i11} to a small saucepan. Cook over medium heat, stirring until a thick, spreadable jam forms. Transfer to a piping bag or small bowl and let cool completely.

steps.title: Shape and fill
steps.content: Preheat oven to 180°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Roll the dough into balls roughly 2cm wide. Press a deep well in the centre of each. Fill each well with a small amount of quince filling — don't overfill.

steps.title: Bake
steps.content: Bake on the middle rack for 10 minutes until the bottoms are golden. The cookies will look slightly underdone on top — they firm up as they cool.

notes: Always use powdered sugar — it gives pepas their characteristic sandy, melt-in-your-mouth texture. Finding quince paste in Amsterdam: check Albert Heijn (Spanish imports section), Marqt, or any Turkish/Moroccan supermarkt. Keeps in an airtight container for up to 5 days — don't refrigerate.

title: Sweet Quinoa Pancakes
description: Fluffy breakfast pancakes with a protein boost from cooked quinoa. Slightly crisp edges, tender inside.

ingredients.name: butter or neutral oil for frying

steps.title: Make the batter
steps.content: Whisk together {i5}, {i6}, {i7}, and {i8} in a bowl. Add {i1} and stir to combine. Fold in {i2}, {i3}, and a pinch of salt until just incorporated — don't overmix, a few lumps are fine.

steps.title: Rest the batter
steps.content: Let the batter sit for 5 minutes while you heat the pan.

steps.title: Cook
steps.content: Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat and melt {i9}. Ladle about 60g of batter per pancake. Cook until bubbles form on the surface and edges look set, about 2–3 minutes. Flip and cook for another 1–2 minutes until golden.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Serve immediately with maple syrup, fresh fruit, or yoghurt.

notes: Works best with day-old cooked quinoa — freshly cooked and still hot can make the batter a bit wet.

title: Quick Cranberry Compote
description: A tart, jammy topping ready in 10 minutes. Great on Bircher, yoghurt, pancakes, or toast.

steps.title: Combine
steps.content: Add {i1}, {i2}, {i3}, the zest and juice of {i4}, and a pinch of cinnamon to a small saucepan over medium heat.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.content: Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring occasionally. The cranberries will start to burst after a few minutes — help them along with the back of a spoon if you like a smoother texture. Cook for about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken and taste
steps.content: Cook until thickened to your liking, another 2–3 minutes. Taste and adjust sweetness with more {i2} if needed. It will thicken further as it cools.

notes: Cranberries are quite tart — don't be shy about adjusting maple syrup at the end. Keeps in the fridge for up to a week.

title: Silken Tofu & Date Pudding
description: A silky, glossy pudding sweetened naturally with Medjool dates. Rich and indulgent without being heavy.

steps.title: Blend base
steps.content: Add {i1}, {i2}, {i3}, {i4}, {i6}, and a pinch of salt to a blender. Blend until completely smooth.

steps.title: Add cornstarch
steps.content: Add {i5} to the blender and blend again briefly to combine cold. This prevents lumps.

steps.title: Cook
steps.content: Pour the mixture into a small saucepan over medium-low heat. Stir constantly — thickening happens suddenly, so stay attentive. Cook until the mixture turns glossy and thick, about 5–8 minutes.

steps.title: Chill
steps.content: Pour into serving glasses or ramekins. Press cling film directly onto the surface to prevent a skin forming. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Serve chilled, topped with sea salt flakes, a drizzle of tahini, or toasted nuts.

notes: Medjool dates are naturally soft and need no soaking. Thickening happens fast once it starts — keep stirring and don't walk away. The pudding sets firmer in the fridge; it will look loose in the pan but that's correct.

title: Bircher Muesli
description: Overnight oats soaked in water kefir — creamy, filling, and ready to grab from the fridge in the morning.

steps.title: Combine
steps.content: Add {i1}, {i2}, and {i3} to a bowl or jar and stir well to combine.

steps.title: Add seeds
steps.content: Stir in {i5}, {i6}, and {i7}. The chia seeds will absorb liquid and help thicken everything overnight.

steps.title: Cover and refrigerate
steps.content: Cover and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 4 hours. The oats will soften and the mixture will thicken.

steps.title: Finish in the morning
steps.content: Grate {i4} directly over the bowl just before eating — grating fresh keeps it from browning. Stir through or layer on top. Add toppings of choice: cranberry compote, honey, or fresh fruit.

notes: Add the flaxseed in the morning rather than the night before to minimise HCN release from the ground flax — a small practical tip. If the muesli is too thick in the morning, loosen with a splash more kefir or yoghurt. Scales easily for batch prep — multiply by 2 or 3 and keep in the fridge for up to 3 days.

title: Hibiscus Water Kefir
description: A fizzy, probiotic fermented drink — lightly tart and naturally carbonated. First ferment base, with hibiscus second ferment.

ingredients.name: non-chlorinated water (let tap water sit 30 min to off-gas chlorine)

steps.title: Make sugar water
steps.content: Dissolve {i3} in a small amount of warm water, then top up to {i1} total with room temperature water. Make sure it is below 30°C before adding grains.

steps.title: Add grains
steps.content: Add {i2} to the sugar water. Cover the jar with a breathable cloth or coffee filter secured with a rubber band — do not use an airtight lid for the first ferment.

steps.title: First ferment (F1)
steps.content: Leave at room temperature for 24–48 hours. At 25°C, check at 24 hours — it should taste lightly tart and less sweet. Strain out the grains and set them aside for your next batch.

steps.title: Second ferment (F2) — hibiscus
steps.content: Add {i4} and {i5} to the strained kefir liquid. Bottle in airtight flip-top bottles, filling to about 80% full. Leave at room temperature for 24–48 hours. At 25°C, 36 hours is usually the sweet spot.

steps.title: Burp and refrigerate
steps.content: Burp the bottles daily during F2 to check carbonation and release pressure. Once nicely fizzy, strain out the hibiscus flowers and refrigerate. Drink within 3–5 days.

notes: Grains: 120g for 1500g water is the recommended ratio (1:10 grains to water). Sugar: 90g gives a 6% solution, which is the sweet spot for grain health. Adjust up to 110g if you want a fuller ferment or your grains are slow. Always use white cane sugar — dark sugars can over-mineralise grains over time. Grains are reusable indefinitely; store in sugar water in the fridge if not brewing immediately.
